runbooks.title: PostgreSQL Latency Spike Playbook
runbooks.content:
## PostgreSQL Latency Spike

### Historical Pattern
This incident has occurred 3 times in the past 60 days. Root cause was always one of:
1. **Missing index on `orders.created_at`** (2 of 3 times) — The analytics daily-rollup query does a full table scan when the date range exceeds 30 days.
2. **Connection pool exhaustion** (1 of 3 times) — The order-service was leaking connections due to unclosed transactions in the retry logic.

### What Worked Last Time (April 15)
1. Killed all queries running > 5000ms
2. Flushed the Redis cache (stale cache was causing retry storms)
3. Added index: `CREATE INDEX idx_orders_created_at ON orders(created_at DESC)`
4. Restarted the analytics-worker with connection pool limit reduced from 50 → 20

### What Did NOT Work
- Rebooting PostgreSQL — made it worse because all connections reconnected simultaneously
- Scaling up read replicas — the bottleneck was on the primary (write locks)

### Prevention
- Set up query timeout at 3000ms in pg_config
- Add circuit breaker to analytics-worker
- Monitor `pg_stat_activity` for connections > 100

runbooks.title: Payment Gateway Failure Recovery
runbooks.content:
## Payment Gateway Failure

### Historical Pattern
Stripe outages have hit us twice in Q1 2026. Both times the pattern was identical:
1. Response times spike from ~150ms to 8-30 seconds
2. Success rate drops below 50% within 3 minutes
3. Error codes: primarily GATEWAY_TIMEOUT and CONNECTION_REFUSED

### What Worked Last Time (April 22)
1. Immediately switched traffic to Adyen fallback (reduced blast radius by 70%)
2. Enabled the payment retry queue with exponential backoff
3. Put up a customer-facing status page banner within 2 minutes
4. Stripe confirmed their side issue and resolved in ~25 minutes

### Critical: Do NOT
- Do NOT retry failed payments automatically without idempotency keys — we double-charged 14 customers last time
- Do NOT disable the payment service entirely — it breaks the cart flow for everyone

### Failover Architecture
- Primary: Stripe (85% of traffic)
- Fallback: Adyen (15% → 100% during Stripe outage)
- Switch command: `kubectl set env deployment/payment-service GATEWAY_PRIMARY=adyen`

runbooks.title: Redis Cache Invalidation Storm
runbooks.content:
## Redis Cache Invalidation Storm

### Pattern
When the product catalog service deploys, it invalidates ALL cache keys at once. This causes a thundering herd to PostgreSQL.

### Fix
1. Use staggered invalidation: `SCAN` + random delay between deletes
2. Pre-warm critical cache keys before invalidation
3. If urgent: restart Redis with `--maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru`

runbooks.title: High CPU on API Gateway
runbooks.content:
## API Gateway CPU Spike

### Root Cause History
- Usually caused by a search indexing job that runs uncapped
- The `/api/search` endpoint has no rate limiting and gets hammered by the mobile app's autocomplete

### Quick Fix
1. Scale API gateway to 4 replicas: `kubectl scale deployment/api-gateway --replicas=4`
2. Enable rate limiting: 100 req/s per IP on `/api/search`
3. Check if the search indexer cron is running during peak hours (it shouldn't be)
